title: Optimization & Quality
scan: What you will learn: The four quality layers every AEM project must enforce — unit, integration, contract, and E2E — plus the Core Web Vitals targets and WCAG AA requirements that are non-negotiable from Sprint 1

Why it matters: Quality is a system property, not a checklist. Without automated enforcement at every layer it degrades to zero within two sprints — because pressure always wins over intention unless the build prevents otherwise
80% coverage with imprecise tests is worse than 60% with precise ones — bad tests produce false confidence and teach the team to ignore failures
LCP and INP have the strongest direct correlation to revenue of the three Core Web Vitals — prioritise them as PR merge gates over CLS, which is more fixable but less business-critical
WCAG 2.2 AA is a contractual obligation on most EU and US public-sector engagements — treating it as optional until week 11 is a project risk, not a design preference

blocks.html: ptimization & Quality 101. Inside CODER, the O letter holds three disciplines that decide whether what you built actually deserves to ship: testing, performance, and accessibility. PMs treat them as separate phases at the end of a project; architects treat them as continuous constraints from day one. This deep-dive covers each — what they ask, where they sit in the engineering loop, and which numbers the team must own.

Three quality gates, three different conversations. Testing asks "does it behave correctly?" Page speed asks "does it behave fast enough?" Accessibility asks "does it behave for everyone?" None of them is optional, and none of them is solved by a tool — each is a discipline of writing code differently from the start.

sections.title: Testing · the pyramid & what each layer asks

blocks.html: Testing · the pyramid & what each layer asks

Testing isn't one thing. It's a stack of confidence-building layers, each cheaper than the one above and each catching different bugs. The classical test pyramid says most tests should be unit, some integration, few end-to-end. Teams violate it all the time and pay for it in slow CI and flaky pipelines.

blocks.title: Quality layers · unit to synthetic monitoring
blocks.source:
flowchart TD
    A["Unit
(Jest / JUnit · AEM Mocks)
hundreds · milliseconds · stable"]
    --> B["Integration
(real DB, real cache, internal calls mocked)
dozens · seconds"]
    --> C["Contract
(Pact · consumer-driven)
service boundaries · cross-team · async"]
    --> D["E2E
(Playwright / Cypress)
critical user journeys only · minutes"]
    --> E["Synthetic Monitoring
(Datadog / Pingdom)
production 24/7 · real DNS · real CDN"]

blocks.html: Contract testing is necessary beyond integration tests. An integration test validates that A and B work together in the test environment today. A contract test validates that B will not change its API in a way that breaks A — even when A and B are tested separately, by different teams, on different schedules. Consumer-driven contracts (Pact) are the correct tool for this. Without contract tests, the integration layer gives you confidence in the present; contract tests give you confidence across time and organisational boundaries.

blocks.title: The test pyramid · base is fastest, apex is slowest

cards.title: One function, no dependencies
cards.body: Pure logic, mocked I/O. Runs in milliseconds. Catches: bad math, wrong branches, off-by-one. Doesn't catch: wiring problems.

cards.title: Multiple units together
cards.body: Real database, real cache, real internal calls — external services still mocked. Catches: schema drift, serialisation bugs, transaction issues. Doesn't catch: end-user flows.

cards.title: The full system, browser to DB
cards.body: Playwright, Cypress, Selenium. Catches: the things only a real user sees. Costs: slow, flaky, expensive to maintain. Keep them few and focused on critical user journeys.

sections.title: Test cases — how to structure one

blocks.html: Test cases — how to structure one
Two patterns. Pick one and apply it everywhere.

columns.label: AAA · Arrange, Act, Assert
columns.body: Three sections, each one to a few lines. Arrange sets up state. Act performs the one operation under test. Assert checks the outcome. If you have two "Acts" in one test, it's two tests. Engineering standard.

columns.label: Given-When-Then
columns.body: BDD flavour. Given the system in state X, when action Y happens, then outcome Z. Reads as specification — better for tests that double as documentation, business-readable acceptance criteria, contract testing.

blocks.html: The single best discipline is to name tests after the bug they prevent:

blocks.label: test names that work as documentation

sections.title: Coverage — a floor, never a goal

blocks.html: Coverage — a floor, never a goal

"100% test coverage" is a meaningless target. Coverage tells you what code ran during the test suite; it doesn't tell you what code is actually verified. You can hit 100% coverage with tests that never assert anything.

items: Set a coverage floor — typically 70–80% line coverage on new code, with branch coverage tracked separately. Fail the build if it drops.
Don't chase 100% — the last 10% is testing trivial getters and framework boilerplate. Diminishing returns, increasing brittleness.

Mutation testing — if you want to know whether your tests are good, not just present. Stryker (JS), Pitest (Java) inject deliberate bugs and verify a test fails. Game-changing for codebases that already have high coverage and still ship bugs.
Coverage on the critical path matters more than overall coverage. Payment flow at 95% beats overall at 85%.

sections.title: Automation — what to automate, what to leave alone

blocks.html: Automation — what to automate, what to leave alone
Three categories of testing work; only one should be automated by default:

cards.title: Regression
cards.body: Anything that has already broken once. Anything on the critical path. Anything you'd otherwise run by hand every release.

cards.title: Smoke & sanity
cards.body: Post-deploy "is the site up" checks. Synthetic monitors do this for you in production; in CI, keep them minimal — under 5 minutes total.

cards.title: Exploratory
cards.body: "Click around and see what feels wrong." Genuinely valuable, genuinely human. Automating exploratory testing is automating the thing you're trying to do, which is to not follow the script.

blocks.html: AEM-specific testing layers

items: AEM Mocks (io.wcm) — the standard mock framework for Sling, JCR, OSGi. Unit tests for Sling Models, Servlets, OSGi services without spinning up a JVM-AEM instance.
HTL unit tests — Sightly Test Framework. Verify that templates render correctly with given Sling Model inputs. Often skipped; should not be.
Integration tests — Cloud Manager pipelines run UI tests against a real AEM instance in CM-Test. Selenium / WebDriver / Playwright as the runner.
Performance tests — JMeter or Gatling, hitting Dispatcher. Test under realistic concurrency, with realistic cache hit ratios — not just cold cache.
Content/migration tests — Groovy scripts on package upload to verify migrated content matches schema. The cheapest way to catch a migration bug before launch.

blocks.html: The CI feedback loop

Tests only catch bugs if developers actually run them. Tests only get run if the loop is fast. Under 10 minutes from push to green is the threshold below which developers trust CI and above which they start ignoring it.

items: Parallelise aggressively — split test suites across runners. Most CI providers charge per minute, not per runner.
Order by speed — unit first, integration next, e2e last. Fail fast on the cheap layers.
Quarantine flakes immediately — a flaky test is worse than no test. It teaches the team to ignore failures.
Cache the slow bits — dependency installs, container images, build artefacts. Re-downloading node_modules on every run is engineering self-harm.

blocks.html: A test suite isn't "done" when it's written. It's done when it has been trusted by the team for six months. The decisions that lead there happen on day one.

sections.title: Page Speed · the score that's not the goal

blocks.html: Page Speed · the score that's not the goal

Page speed is the user-visible side of CODER's O letter. Caching (in Deployment 101 above) is the infrastructure-side answer; this is the application-side answer. They reinforce each other — neither alone is enough.
Core Web Vitals — what Google measures, what users feel
Three numbers, each tied to a phase of the user's experience:

cards.title: Largest Contentful Paint
cards.body: How long until the biggest visible element renders. Hero image or H1, usually. Good: < 2.5s. The classic "page feels slow" metric.

cards.title: Interaction to Next Paint
cards.body: How long after a click/tap does the page respond visually. Replaced FID in 2024. Good: < 200ms. Reveals JS-heavy event handlers.

cards.title: Cumulative Layout Shift
cards.body: How much the page jumps around as it loads. Banners, late-loading ads, web fonts. Good: < 0.1. The most fixable, most ignored vital.

blocks.html: DEPT® field pattern — set a Lighthouse CI performance budget of LCP ≤ 2500ms and INP ≤ 200ms in every project's CI configuration. Make it a PR merge gate from Sprint 1, not a post-launch audit. A regression caught in a PR takes 10 minutes to fix; the same regression caught in production takes a release cycle.

sections.title: Lab vs field & the critical rendering path

blocks.html: Lab vs field — Lighthouse lies, CrUX tells

columns.label: Lab (Lighthouse)
columns.body: Single run, controlled conditions, throttled to a slow phone. Useful for catching regressions in CI. Does not reflect what real users experience — your users aren't all on a Moto G4.

columns.label: Field (CrUX / RUM)
columns.body: Real measurements from real browsers in the wild, aggregated by Google's CrUX dataset or your own RUM. This is what Google uses for ranking. Believe this number.

blocks.html: The critical rendering path

The browser needs HTML, CSS, and (sometimes) JS before it can paint. Every blocking resource extends time-to-first-paint. The optimisation is conceptually simple: defer everything that isn't on the critical path.

items: HTML first — server-rendered HTML lets the browser start parsing immediately. SPAs that render entirely in JS lose this.
Critical CSS inlined — the CSS needed for above-the-fold rendering goes in a <style> tag in <head>. Everything else is async.
JS deferred — defer or async on every script. Scripts in <head> without one of these are render-blocking.
Third-party tags last — analytics, chat, A/B, tag managers. Measure their cost in milliseconds; you'll be horrified.

blocks.html: Running load tests only in staging environments with synthetic data. Real production load patterns have long-tail requests, warm-up cache characteristics, and third-party script load behaviour that staging cannot replicate. Run load tests against production on a maintenance window before go-live — with real CDN, real DNS, real traffic routing.
Images — the heaviest payload, the biggest win
Images are usually 50–70% of page weight. They're also where the biggest wins live, and the wins are mostly mechanical.

items: Format · AVIF > WebP > JPEG > PNG. AVIF saves another 30% over WebP. Use <picture> with format fallbacks.
Responsive · srcset + sizes. Mobile gets a smaller file; desktop gets the right resolution. AEM image renditions automate this; EDS does it natively.
Lazy-load · loading="lazy" on every below-the-fold image. Don't lazy-load the LCP image — that's a common own goal.
Width/height attributes · always. The browser reserves the space before the image downloads, eliminating CLS from images.

blocks.html: JavaScript — the budget

The single best tool an engineering lead has is a JS budget per route. Above the budget, the build fails. The number depends on the audience; common starting points: 170 KB compressed for marketing sites, 250 KB for app-heavy interfaces.

items: Code splitting — load only what's needed for the current route. Webpack, Vite, esbuild all do this. Default for Next.js and modern frameworks.
Tree shaking — drop unused exports at build time. Watch out for libraries that ship as side-effectful modules; they kill tree shaking.
Audit dependencies — every npm install is a perf decision. Bundlephobia exists. Use it.
The third-party tag problem — that one tag manager adds 800ms to LCP. Move tags server-side where possible (Edge tag managers, server-side GTM).

blocks.html: Fonts — the silent CLS culprit

items: Preload critical fonts — <link rel="preload" as="font" crossorigin>.
font-display: swap — show a fallback while the custom font loads. Users see text immediately.
Subset — ship only the glyphs you use. A 200 KB font becomes 30 KB. Variable fonts (one file, many weights) often win overall.
System font stack for body copy is a legitimate choice — zero font payload, zero CLS, native feel.

blocks.html: AEM-specific levers

items: Dispatcher cache rules — covered in Deployment 101. The biggest LCP lever on AEM is hitting Dispatcher cache, not Publisher.

EDS (Edge Delivery Services) — Adobe's decoupled-authoring path. Eliminates the heavy HTL render entirely. CLS and LCP scores typically jump 30+ points moving from classic AEM to EDS for the same content.
Image renditions — pre-generate srcset variants at upload time. Dispatcher serves them directly.
Client libs (clientlibs) — concatenate, minify, version. Mind the order: don't load author-only libs on publish.
Lazy authoring — components below the fold render on demand, not on page request.

blocks.html: Page speed isn't a phase. It's a budget. Every commit either spends from it or earns it back. Teams that publish their budgets on a wall hit them; teams that don't, don't.

sections.title: Accessibility · WCAG as the agency floor

blocks.html: Accessibility · WCAG as the agency floor

The third quality gate. Often the one teams retrofit at the end, which is the most expensive way to do it. Accessibility designed in costs almost nothing; accessibility retrofitted into a launched system costs a redesign.

Before

Accessibility audit in week 11 of a 12-week project. 40 critical WCAG issues found; 18 require component redesign; go-live delayed 3 weeks.

After

axe-core integrated in Storybook from Sprint 1. Zero critical issues at launch. Accessibility is a zero-sprint-overhead continuous check, not a 3-week remediation sprint.
WCAG — what to target
WCAG 2.2 Level AA is the agency floor. AAA is for specialist contexts (government, regulated industries with accessibility mandates). AA covers what 95% of users actually need.
WCAG organises around four principles (POUR):

items: Perceivable — text alternatives for images, captions for video, sufficient contrast, content that works when the page is zoomed 200%.
Operable — every interaction works from the keyboard, no traps, focus visible at all times, skip-links to bypass nav.
Understandable — language declared, error messages clear, forms labelled, behaviour predictable.
Robust — semantic HTML, valid markup, ARIA only where native semantics aren't enough.

blocks.html: The seven 80% wins
Most accessibility complaints come from a small set of fixable things. Get these right and most users stop being blocked:

items: Semantic HTML — <button> not <div onclick>. <nav>, <main>, <article>. Free behaviour, free accessibility.
Alt text on every image — descriptive, not decorative ("CFO Maria Hernandez at quarterly call", not "image1.jpg"). Decorative images get alt="", not omitted alt.
Form labels — every input has a <label>, programmatically associated. Placeholder text is not a label.
Keyboard reachability — tab through the page. Every interactive element reachable, in a logical order, with visible focus state.
Colour contrast — 4.5:1 for body text, 3:1 for large text. Free tools will catch this in CI.
Heading hierarchy — one <h1> per page. <h2> children, <h3> grandchildren. Screen reader users navigate by headings.
Focus management — when a modal opens, focus moves into it; when it closes, focus returns to the trigger. The thing every JS dev forgets.

blocks.html: ARIA · where it helps, where it harms
The first rule of ARIA is: don't use ARIA if a native element works. Bad ARIA is worse than no ARIA — it overrides native behaviour with broken substitutes.

columns.label: Wrong
columns.body: <div role="button" tabindex="0" onclick> — reinventing a button. Doesn't handle Enter/Space. Doesn't work in form submission. Pure tax.

columns.label: Right
columns.body: <button>. Built-in keyboard behaviour, form participation, focus handling. Free.

blocks.html: Where ARIA is essential: dynamic content (live regions for status updates), complex widgets that don't exist in HTML (tab panels, comboboxes, trees), and labelling visual-only context (icon-only buttons need aria-label).
Tooling

items: axe-core — automated rule engine. Catches roughly 30–40% of issues. Run in CI; never trust as the only check.
Lighthouse accessibility — quick spot check. Same engine as axe under the hood.
Screen reader testing — NVDA (Windows, free), VoiceOver (macOS, built in). 30 minutes per major flow catches things axe can't see.
Keyboard-only testing — unplug your mouse for a day. The cheapest, most effective audit available.
User testing with assistive tech users — the only thing that catches real-world failure. Budget for it; don't pretend it's optional.

blocks.html: Accessibility is a competence, not a checklist. Tools catch the obvious; the rest is taught, reviewed, and shipped by people who care. If no one on the team is responsible for it, no one will be doing it.

blocks.label: Architect's Review · Optimization & Quality
items: Is there a test pyramid in shape — many unit, some integration, few e2e?
What is the coverage floor, and is it enforced on PRs?
Does CI run in under 10 minutes? If not, what's the parallelisation plan?
Are flaky tests quarantined the same day they flake, or tolerated?
For AEM: are AEM Mocks tests written for every Sling Model and Servlet?
Are LCP / INP / CLS targets defined and visible to the team?
Is there a JS budget per route, enforced in the build?
What's the cache hit ratio at the edge — and what does the LCP look like at a miss?
Is WCAG 2.2 AA the agreed floor, and is it tested in CI plus screen reader plus keyboard?
Is there a named person accountable for accessibility on this engagement?
